summary: Phase 10c - Personal data export (LFPDPPP ARCO-A) GET /api/users/me/export-data: 7/7 backend tests pass. Verified (1) unauth -> 401; (2) headers: Content-Type application/json; charset=utf-8, Content-Disposition attachment with filename bookvia-mis-datos-<id8>-YYYY-MM-DD.json, Cache-Control no-store; (3) body is a JSON string (Response) with meta {generated_at ISO, generated_ip (first X-Forwarded-For hop = '9.9.9.9'), schema_version='1.0', user_id, note ES}; top-level keys: profile, bookings[], wallet, payments[], notifications[], favorites[], terms_acceptance_history[]; (4) sanitization -- profile does NOT leak password_hash, email_verification_token, reset_password_token, totp_secret, _id; payments do NOT leak stripe_secret; no collection contains _id; (5) client booking present with id, business_id, date, status; (6) wallet.balance_mxn propagated; payments[] has seeded row; (7) terms_acceptance_history returned as list with version/accepted_at/source; (8) business OWNER (role=business, is_manager=False, business_id set) additionally gets business_profile, business_settlements[], business_transactions[], business_strikes[]; client regular does NOT get those keys; (9) business MANAGER (is_manager=True) currently DOES get the business_* keys because the router gates only on role==BUSINESS + business_id (documented in test as 'follows user.business_id'); (10) db.audit_logs row inserted with action='personal_data_export', actor_id==target_id==user_id, ip='7.7.7.7' (first XFF hop), details.bookings/payments/notifications counts >=1, is_business=False for client; (11) token is authoritative -- export always returns the token owner's data (no /other_id path exists).
critical_code_review_comments: routers/users.py export_my_personal_data: the business_* branch gates on `role==BUSINESS and business_id`, so a MANAGER (is_manager=True) who has business_id on their user doc will receive business_profile/settlements/transactions/strikes of the managed business. If the intent of LFPDPPP Acceso is 'datos del titular' only, a manager arguably should not download the business's settlements/strikes (those belong to the owner entity). Consider adding `and not user.get('is_manager')` to restrict to owners only, or explicitly documenting the current behavior in the privacy policy. Test file currently asserts present-behavior (keys present for manager) so bumping this will require updating test_business_manager_behavior_follows_business_id.

routers/users.py _sanitize_doc drops a fixed allowlist. It will NOT scrub nested sensitive fields (e.g., if a booking row stores raw card_fingerprint, or a payment_transactions doc accrues a new secret field in the future). Consider an allowlist-based projection per collection instead of a denylist, or at minimum a regex sweep on keys ending in _secret/_token/_hash when recursing.

routers/users.py bookings/notifications/payments cap at 500 rows via .to_list(500) with no pagination. A long-tenure business or power user could exceed 500 and silently miss rows in their export. LFPDPPP requires completeness; either raise the cap (500 -> unbounded with server-side streaming) or add a top-level 'truncated: true' flag in meta when the cap is hit.

routers/users.py audit log writes created_at as an ISO string (`generated_at`), but other audit_logs in the codebase typically store BSON datetime. This inconsistency will break sort/range queries on audit_logs.created_at. Consider writing datetime.now(timezone.utc) (object, not isoformat) so Mongo stores BSON date.

routers/users.py filename uses `user['id'][:8]` which is fine for uuid-style ids but produces ambiguous filenames like `bookvia-mis-datos-TEST_F10-2026-01-16.json` when ids carry underscores. Minor UX issue; consider stripping non-alphanumerics or using a hash.

routers/users.py Content-Disposition filename is not RFC 5987-encoded (no filename*=UTF-8''...). If a user.id ever contains non-ASCII, browsers on some platforms will fall back to an ugly default name. Current ids are ASCII so low risk today.

routers/users.py writes db.audit_logs with a bespoke dict; the rest of the codebase uses helpers.create_audit_log. Using the helper keeps action/target enum values in sync (AuditAction) and centralizes the schema.
test_credentials: JWT minted directly with HS256+JWT_SECRET from /app/backend/.env. No TOTP / admin login flow needed. Seed users are TEST_F10C_*_<RUN> with the three role combinations (client, owner is_manager=False, manager is_manager=True).
seed_data_creation: TEST_F10C_ prefix + per-run uuid: 3 users (client, owner, manager), 2 businesses (owner, manager), 1 booking, 1 payment_transaction (with stripe_secret to test sanitization), 1 notification, 1 user_favorite, 1 user_wallet (balance_mxn=123.45), 1 settlement, 1 transaction, 1 business_strike. All cleaned up in fixture teardown across 10 collections + audit_logs (by id-prefix OR user_id/business_id/actor_id prefix).
context_for_next_testing_agent: test_fase10c_data_export.py is idempotent (TEST_F10C_ prefix + per-run RUN uuid). Endpoint returns a raw Response body (json.dumps string), so tests use r.text + json.loads AFTER header validation (never r.json()). IP audit assertion depends on X-Forwarded-For being forwarded by the ingress to the backend (it is in this environment). If a future change switches to an allowlist-style sanitization per collection, the test_client_payload_shape_and_sanitization may need to be relaxed (currently asserts existence of seeded fields like wallet.balance_mxn, booking.service_name). Manager-behavior test currently locks in the 'manager sees business_*' contract; if the main agent tightens the gate to owners-only, flip that test to assert business_profile NOT in data.
